fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need a spell-checking CLI tool for our source repos. The idea is pretty straightforward — we've been accumulating tons of typos in comments and docs across multiple codebases and it's becoming embarrassing when external contributors notice them. We want something that only flags words we *know* are wrong (no false positives from identifiers or jargon), and can optionally just fix them automatically in-place.

Basically it should scan files or whole directories, tell you where the typos are (file + line), and suggest corrections. When fixing automatically it should be smart about capitalization — like if someone typed a word in all-caps it should fix it in all-caps too, same deal with title case.

We also need ways to suppress certain words from being reported (both inline and from a file), skip certain files by pattern, and handle weird encodings gracefully. There's also that summary mode we talked about in the last sprint — same approach we used for the login module audit tool, so refer to that for how the tallying should look.

One thing I keep forgetting to mention: the quiet flag behavior — it's a bitmask thing, not just a boolean. Make sure whoever implements this checks how bit 16 specifically is supposed to work with the file-rewrite notifications. Also need proper error handling if someone points it at a dictionary file that doesn't exist. The word-boundary logic matters too — by default underscores should keep things glued together.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: def check(files: dict[str, str], args: list[str], stdin: str | None = None, show_files: list[str] | None = None) -> str

trigger_keywords: how are findings formatted
what does a report line look like
output format for misspellings
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each finding in file content is printed as `<path>:<line>: <wrong>  ==> <fix>` — note exactly two spaces before `==>`. A finding in a file's name (when filename-checking is enabled) is printed as `<path>: <wrong>  ==> <fix>` with no line number. The total is always printed as `count=<n>` on its own line at the end.

trigger_keywords: how should capitalization be matched
case preservation rules
what casing logic applies to suggestions
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The correction must mirror the casing of the original misspelled word using exactly four rules: (1) all lowercase → correction is all lowercase; (2) title case (only first letter capitalized) → correction is title-cased; (3) all uppercase → correction is all uppercase; (4) any other mixed casing → fall back to the plain lowercase correction from the dictionary.

trigger_keywords: quiet flag behavior
what does the -q option do
how does the bitmask suppress output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The `-q <n>` option is a numeric bitmask. Bit 16 (value 16) specifically controls whether the `fixed=<name>` per-file notification is emitted when writing changes in place. When bit 16 is set (e.g. `-q 16`), the file is still rewritten but the `fixed=` line is suppressed. Without that bit the notification is printed normally. Other bits may control other message categories.

trigger_keywords: what does the summary section look like
summary format details
how is the tally printed
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When `--summary` or `-s` is enabled, after all per-finding lines the tool prints: a blank line, then `-------8<-------`, then `SUMMARY:`, then one line per distinct misspelled word (keyed by lowercase form, sorted alphabetically) in the format `<word>    <count>` with right-aligned numeric padding. If nothing was found the section still appears but with no word lines. The `count=<n>` line follows the summary.

trigger_keywords: default word boundary pattern
what counts as a word by default
how are words split from text
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: By default, words are identified by a pattern that includes alphanumeric characters plus underscore, hyphen, and apostrophe as word-internal characters. This means a misspelling joined to other text by an underscore (e.g. `abandonned_foo`) is NOT isolated and will NOT be reported. A custom regex can be supplied with `-r <regex>` to redefine word boundaries — for example `[a-z]+` would break on underscores and expose embedded misspellings.

trigger_keywords: how are binary files handled
what happens with non-text files
encoding fallback behavior
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Files are decoded as UTF-8, falling back to Latin-1. A file containing a null byte is treated as binary and skipped entirely without spell-checking. Skipping a binary file emits `warning=binary_file` on its own line, and the count for that file is zero because no content was checked. Valid non-ASCII UTF-8 content (e.g. accented characters) is read correctly and not flagged.

trigger_keywords: what happens with a bad dictionary path
missing dictionary error handling
error when dictionary file not found
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If a custom dictionary is supplied via `-D <path>` and that path does not exist, the run is aborted before any file is checked. The adapter emits `error=dictionary_not_found` followed by `count=1` (the non-zero exit status). No spell-check report or finding lines are produced.

trigger_keywords: how does write-changes work
in-place fix behavior
what does -w do to files
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: With `-w`, instead of only reporting, the engine replaces every detected misspelling with its case-matched correction directly in the file. Each rewritten file produces a `fixed=<name>` line (unless suppressed by quiet bit 16). The `count=` value after a full in-place fix run is 0, because everything detected was corrected. The original line structure (newlines) is preserved.

trigger_keywords: how are hidden files handled
dot files behavior
skipping files starting with a dot
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Files whose names begin with a dot are treated as hidden and are skipped by default — misspellings inside them are not reported and they do not contribute to the count. The `--check-hidden` flag enables checking of hidden files exactly like any other file.

trigger_keywords: how does the skip option work
excluding files by pattern
what does --skip do
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The `--skip=<patterns>` option accepts a comma-separated list of shell-style wildcard patterns. When scanning a directory, any file whose name matches one of those patterns is excluded entirely from checking — no findings are reported for it and it does not contribute to the count.

trigger_keywords: how does filename checking work
check-filenames option behavior
reporting misspellings in file names
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: By default only file contents are checked. With `-f` (or `--check-filenames`), the words making up each file's name are also checked against the dictionary. A misspelling found in a filename is reported as `<path>: <wrong>  ==> <fix>` — no line number, because the match is in the name not the content.

trigger_keywords: how does inline ignore list work
what is the -L option
combining multiple ignore lists
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The `-L <words>` option accepts a comma-separated list of words to never report. It may be supplied multiple times and all lists are combined. Words on the combined ignore list are silently skipped; all other dictionary matches are still reported. Lookup is case-insensitive.

trigger_keywords: exclude lines file behavior
what does -x do
skipping specific lines from checking
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The `-x <path>` option points to a file listing whole lines of text to skip. Any line in the target file whose full text exactly matches a line in the exclude file is skipped entirely — misspellings on that line are not reported. Lines not listed in the exclude file are checked normally.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: same approach we used for the login module audit tool, so refer to that for how the tallying should look
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The summary section format defined in Feature 4: a blank line, `-------8<-------`, `SUMMARY:` heading, then alphabetically sorted word-count lines formatted as `<word>    <count>` with right-aligned padding, followed by `count=<n>`.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the quiet flag behavior — it's a bitmask thing, not just a boolean. Make sure whoever implements this checks how bit 16 specifically is supposed to work
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The `-q <n>` numeric bitmask where bit value 16 suppresses the `fixed=<name>` per-file notification line when writing changes in place, as specified in Feature 3.2 quiet suppress behavior.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect. Please check the PRD (start.md) and the public test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the precise behavioral contract — the test JSON files are the authoritative specification for each feature's expected input/output.

feature1_detect_report.json.description: Run the spell checker over the textual content of a single file using default settings and report what it finds. For each detected misspelling the tool emits one report line giving the file name, the one-based line number where the word occurs, the misspelled word, and its suggested correction. After the report lines a summary line states the total number of misspellings found. A file whose content contains no recognised misspellings produces no report lines and a total of zero.

feature2_case_preserving.json.description: Match the capitalization of each suggested correction to the capitalization of the misspelled word that was found. When the misspelled word is entirely lower case the suggestion is lower case; when it is in title case the suggestion is title-cased; when it is fully upper case the suggestion is upper-cased; and when its capitalization follows no recognised pattern the suggestion falls back to the plain lower-case correction. Each detection is reported as one line giving the file name, the one-based line number, the misspelled word, and the case-matched correction, followed by a count of detections.

cases.expected_output:
a.txt:1: abandonned  ==> abandoned
a.txt:2: Abandonned  ==> Abandoned
a.txt:3: ABANDONNED  ==> ABANDONED
a.txt:4: AbAnDoNnEd  ==> abandoned
count=4

feature3_1_write_changes.json.description: When the write-changes flag is supplied, the tool rewrites each affected file in place, replacing every misspelled word with its case-matched correction instead of only reporting it. Each rewritten file is announced with a neutral notification carrying the file name, the total count of remaining misspellings drops to zero, and the resulting file content reflects every applied correction with the original line structure preserved.

cases.expected_output:
fixed=a.txt
count=0
file=a.txt
abandoned
Abandoned
ABANDONED
abandoned

feature3_2_quiet_suppress.json.description: The quiet-level option is a numeric bitmask that suppresses selected categories of informational messages. With the bit that controls fixed-file notifications enabled, rewriting files in place still applies every correction but no longer emits the per-file fixed notification, whereas without that bit the notification is emitted. The two scenarios share identical correction results and identical resulting file content; they differ only in whether the fixed-file notification line is present.

feature4_summary.json.description: When the summary option is enabled, after the normal per-finding report the tool appends a summary section: a separator line, a SUMMARY heading, and then one line per distinct misspelled word giving the word followed by right-aligned padding and the number of times that word was found, sorted by word. When nothing was found the summary section is present but lists no words. The total-count line follows the summary as usual.

cases.expected_output:
a.txt:1: abandonned  ==> abandoned
a.txt:2: abandonned  ==> abandoned

-------8<-------
SUMMARY:
abandonned    2
count=2

feature5_1_ignore_words_inline.json.description: Words supplied through the inline ignore-list option are treated as acceptable and are never reported as misspellings. The option takes a comma-separated list of words and may be supplied more than once, with all supplied lists combined. With no ignore list every recognised misspelling in the file is reported; when some of those words are placed on the ignore list they are skipped and only the remaining misspellings are reported and counted.

cases.expected_output:
a.txt:1: abandonned  ==> abandoned
a.txt:2: abondon  ==> abandon
a.txt:3: abilty  ==> ability
count=3

feature5_2_ignore_words_file.json.description: An ignore-words file lists, one word per line, words that must never be reported as misspellings. When the file is supplied, words appearing in it are skipped while all other misspellings in the checked file are still reported and counted; without the ignore file those same words are reported normally.

cases.expected_output:
bad.txt:1: abandonned  ==> abandoned
bad.txt:2: abondon  ==> abandon
count=2

feature5_3_custom_word_pattern.json.description: By default the tool builds candidate words using a pattern that treats alphanumeric characters together with the underscore, hyphen and apostrophe as part of a word, so a misspelling joined to other text by an underscore is not isolated and therefore not reported. A custom word-matching regular expression can be supplied to redefine what counts as a word; supplying a pattern that breaks on underscores isolates the embedded misspellings so they are detected.

cases.expected_output:
a.txt:1: abandonned  ==> abandoned
a.txt:1: abondon  ==> abandon
count=2

feature5_4_exclude_lines.json.description: An exclude file lists whole lines of text that should be left unchecked. Any line in the target file whose full text exactly matches a line in the exclude file is skipped entirely, so misspellings on that line are not reported; lines that are not listed are checked normally.

cases.expected_output:
bad.txt:1: abandonned  ==> abandoned
bad.txt:2: abandonned  ==> abandoned
count=2

feature6_1_skip_patterns.json.description: When a directory is checked, the skip option excludes files whose names match any of a comma-separated list of patterns, where patterns may use shell-style wildcards. A clean file produces no findings; a file containing a misspelling is reported with its path relative to the checked directory unless its name matches a skip pattern, in which case it is omitted entirely.

feature6_2_check_filenames.json.description: By default only file contents are checked. When the check-filenames option is enabled, the words that make up each file's name are also checked against the correction dictionary, and a misspelling in a file name is reported on its own line giving the file path and the suggested correction (without a line number, since the match is in the name rather than in the content).

feature6_3_check_hidden.json.description: Files whose name begins with a dot are treated as hidden and are skipped by default, so a misspelling inside such a file is not reported. When the check-hidden option is enabled, hidden files are checked like any other file. The total count reflects everything that was checked.

feature7_encoding_binary.json.description: Text files are decoded as UTF-8 (falling back to Latin-1), so content containing non-ASCII letters is read correctly and a word with valid accented characters is not reported as a misspelling. Files detected as binary (those containing a null byte) are skipped without being spell-checked; by default skipping a binary file emits a neutral binary-file warning, and in every binary case the total count is zero because no content was checked.

feature8_missing_dictionary.json.description: A custom correction dictionary can be supplied by path. When the supplied dictionary path does not exist, the run is aborted before any file is checked and the tool reports a neutral configuration error indicating that the dictionary could not be found, rather than producing any spell-check report. The accompanying total is the non-zero error status.
